book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
title: The Prosphoneticus to the Emperor.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: (Labbe and Cossart, Concilia, Tom.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: 1047 et seqq.)

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: But because the adversary Satan allows no rest, he has raised up the very ministers of Christ against him, as if armed and carrying weapons, etc.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: Things being so, it was necessary that your beloved of Christ majesty should gather together this all holy, and numerous assembly.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: Thereafter being inspired by the Holy Ghost, and all agreeing and consenting together, and giving our approval to the doctrinal letter of our most blessed and exalted pope, Agatho, which he sent to your mightiness, as also agreeing to the suggestion of the holy synod of one hundred and twenty-five fathers held under him, we teach that one of the Holy Trinity, our Lord Jesus Christ, was incarnate, and must be celebrated in two perfect natures without division and without confusion.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For as the Word, he is consubstantial and eternal with God his father; but as taking flesh of the immaculate Virgin Mary, the Mother of God, he is perfect man, consubstantial with us and made in time.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: We declare therefore that he is perfect in Godhead and that the same is perfect likewise in manhood, according to the pristine tradition of the fathers and the divine definition of Chalcedon.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: And as we recognize two natures, so also we recognize two natural wills and two natural operations.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For we dare not say that either of the natures which are in Christ in his incarnation is without a will and operation: lest in taking away the proprieties of those natures, we likewise take away the natures of which they are the proprieties.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For we neither deny the natural will of his humanity, or its natural operation: lest we also deny what is the chief thing of the dispensation for our salvation, and lest we attribute passions to the Godhead.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For this they were attempting who have recently introduced the detestable novelty that in him there is but one will and one operation, renewing the malignancy of Arius, Apollinaris, Eutyches and Severus.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For should we say that the human nature of our Lord is without will and operation, how could we affirm in safety the perfect humanity?

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For nothing else constitutes the integrity of human nature except the essential will, through which the strength of free-will is marked in us; and this is also the case with the substantial operation.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For how shall we call him perfect in humanity if he in no wise suffered and acted as a man?

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For like as the union of two natures preserves for us one subsistence without confusion and without division; so this one subsistence, shewing itself in two natures, demonstrates as its own what things belong to each.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: Therefore we declare that in him there are two natural wills and two natural operations, proceeding commonly and without division: but we cast out of the Church and rightly subject to anathema all superfluous novelties as well as their inventors: to wit, Theodore of Pharan, Sergius and Paul, Pyrrhus, and Peter (who were archbishops of Constantinople), moreover Cyrus, who bore the priesthood of Alexandria, and with them Honorius, who was the ruler (πρόεδρον) of Rome, as he followed them in these things.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: Besides these, with the best of cause we anathematize and depose Macarius, who was bishop of Antioch, and his disciple Stephen (or rather we should say master), who tried to defend the impiety of their predecessors, and in short stirred up the whole world, and by their pestilential letters and by their fraudulent institutions devastated multitudes in every direction.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: Likewise also that old man Polychronius, with an infantile intelligence, who promised he would raise the dead and who when they did not rise, was laughed at; and all who have taught, or do teach, or shall presume to teach one will and one operation in the incarnate Christ.…But the highest prince of the Apostles fought with us: for we had on our side his imitator and the successor in his see, who also had set forth in his letter the mystery of the divine word (θεολογίας).

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For the ancient city of Rome handed thee a confession of divine character, and a chart from the sunsetting raised up the day of dogmas, and made the darkness manifest, and Peter spoke through Agatho, and thou, O autocratic King, according to the divine decree, with the Omnipotent Sharer of thy throne, didst judge.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: But, O benign and justice-loving Lord, do thou in return do this favour to him who hath bestowed thy power upon thee; and give, as a seal to what has been defined by us, thy imperial ratification in writing, and so confirm them with the customary pious edicts and constitutions, that no one may contradict the things which have been done, nor raise any fresh question.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: For rest assured, O serene majesty, that we have not falsified anything defined by the Ecumenical Councils and by the approved fathers, but we have confirmed them.

verses.book: The Sixth Ecumenical Council. The Third Council of Constantinople.
verses.text: And now we all cry out with one mind and one voice, “O God, save the King! etc., etc.”
